annotations.comment: @intrinsic(role) / @preamble(target) / @builtin(name) / @extern / @environment / @keep — the compiler-contract layer, same family as kernel/user/process/thread. Like @extern/@environment, @keep takes no parenthesized argument (it's a bare marker exempting a class/module/free-func from Dce+Densifier), so it follows the same no-args spelling. @builtin, like @intrinsic/@preamble, requires a parenthesized name argument (Lexer.cs's ReadParenArg) — it is a class/module annotation, not a no-args marker.

patterns.comment: The (role) group is wrapped as ONE optional unit, not three independently-optional captures — @extern (no parens) used to let the next word (e.g. 'func') match capture 3 as if it were a role name, since an unparenthesized identifier alone satisfied that capture with no paren in sight.

brackets.comment: Parens and square brackets — shared by every context that has them (top-level code, raw native C bodies, and risk regions alike), so the rule is exactly the same everywhere: yellow, except a literal empty pair '()' (a no-arg call/ctor — nothing to draw attention to), which renders grey instead — UNLESS that empty pair is immediately preceded by ']' (e.g. 'new List[V]()'), where the '()' is completing a generic instantiation rather than standing alone, so it stays yellow like everything else. Square brackets have no empty-pair exception of their own. Order matters: the empty-pair match must be tried before the general single-character one, or it'd never win.

nativeInner.comment: Raw C spliced verbatim by the compiler — greyed out uniformly, NO sub-highlighting at all (not even parens/brackets — this is the one place that's deliberately fully flat, unlike #riskBody). Mirrors the lexer's ReadBalanced brace-counter exactly: a `{`/`}` inside a `//` line comment, a `/* */` block comment, or a "..."/'...' string/char literal must NOT be counted as a real brace, or a literal `}` in any of those (e.g. `char* s = "closing brace }";`) desyncs the counter and ends the native block early — same hazard the lexer itself was hardened against. Comments and string/char literals are matched FIRST (before the brace-counting alternatives) so they're consumed whole and their internal braces never reach the brace rules at all.

nativeBlocks.comment: native { ... } and native type Name { ... } — 'native'/'type' and the delimiting braces are all the risk surface (same maroon as unsafe/throws/panic, since this is the C escape hatch); the type name (if any) stays a class name; only the raw C *content* between the braces gets the flat grey treatment.

fieldsBlock.comment: fields { ... } — a raw C struct-field block inside a class; same greyed-out treatment as native{}, braces included.

patterns.comment: Interpolated string: $"...{expr}..." — text stays string-colored; each {expr} hole is fully re-highlighted as ordinary code. `{{`/`}}` are literal-brace escapes (mirrors the lexer's ReadInterp) and MUST be matched before the single-`{` hole-begin rule, or e.g. `{{braces}}` would be misread as a hole starting with a stray `{`.
patterns.name: string.interpolated.gata

patterns.comment: Hex int, with the same u/U/l/L suffix run the lexer accepts.
patterns.name: constant.numeric.integer.hex.gata

patterns.comment: Float: decimal point and/or exponent, optional f/F suffix.
patterns.name: constant.numeric.float.gata

unsafeBlock.comment: unsafe { ... } — unlike native{}, the body is ordinary Gata code (parsed as a real Block, not raw C), so ONLY the keyword and its OWN top-level delimiting braces get the risk color; the contents fully tokenize like normal code via #riskBody, including parens/brackets, which follow the exact same #brackets rule as everywhere else (not risk red — only the block's own { } are). The end pattern is plain '\}', which is only safe because every '{' encountered inside #riskBody is intercepted by #riskBraceBlock first (never falls through to a bare brace match) — so by construction the first unescorted '}' this rule's own end can see really is the one that closes THIS block, not some inner if/while body. (A naive version without that interception would prematurely match the first nested '}' it found, e.g. the close of an inner if-block, well before the real end of the unsafe block.)

riskBraceBlock.comment: Any nested `{ ... }` (if/while/for/function body, ...) encountered while already inside a risk region. Recurses through #riskBody for its contents and consumes its own matching '}' here, so the enclosing unsafeBlock's bare '\}' end pattern never sees this brace at all — that's what makes nesting safe instead of closing the outer block early.

riskPunctuation.comment: Punctuation while inside a risk region (unsafe{}) — identical to #punctuation, including parens/brackets (#brackets, same rule, same colors, no special-casing for being inside unsafe{}); the only actual difference is that bare '{' is routed through #riskBraceBlock instead of being matched as a flat single character (see #riskBraceBlock for why that distinction matters).

riskBody.comment: The same token set as the file's top-level patterns, used inside unsafe{} instead of $self — identical in every way (including recursing into nested unsafe{}/native{}/fields{} normally, and including parens/brackets coloring) except it includes #riskPunctuation instead of #punctuation, so nested braces route through #riskBraceBlock for correct depth tracking instead of being flat single-character matches.

riskKeywords.comment: Gata's own risk surface: the only language constructs that can break safety/abort the kernel. Deliberately the one keyword category that isn't just 'a keyword color' — it's a warning.
riskKeywords.name: keyword.control.risk.gata

storageKeywords.comment: Declaration/storage keywords — the same bucket real C# colors for class/struct/static/var (Dark+'s 'storage'/'storage.type' scope): they declare shape, they don't transfer control. Includes the realm/topology and native/fields keywords too — Dark+ has no equivalent category for those.
storageKeywords.name: keyword.control.gata

controlFlowKeywords.comment: Branching/looping/control-transfer — if/else/while/for/switch/case/break/continue/... 'return' and 'default' live here too: 'return' is a control-transfer statement like break/continue, not a declaration; 'default' is overwhelmingly used as a switch/match case label (pairs visually with 'case'), even though it also appears in the rarer 'default(Type)' value-expression form.
controlFlowKeywords.name: keyword.control.flow.gata

genericParam.comment: A bare single uppercase letter (+ optional digits) — T, K, V, U, T1 — Gata's own convention for a generic parameter, as opposed to a real (multi-letter PascalCase) type name.
genericParam.name: entity.name.type.parameter.gata

className.comment: Multi-letter PascalCase — String, List, Console, a user class. Excludes one shape: PascalCaseIdent( with no preceding 'new' — that's a method/function being declared or called (Main(), Console.PrintLine(...)), not a type reference, so it falls through to plainIdentifier (default white) instead. 'new Foo(...)' is exempted from the exclusion since a constructor call is still very much a type reference.

selfKeyword.comment: Not a real lexer token (the resolver special-cases the plain identifier 'self'), but it deserves the same treatment most languages give this/self — same family as the storage/control keywords.
selfKeyword.name: variable.language.self.gata

functionName.comment: Any identifier immediately followed by ( that wasn't already claimed by a keyword (if/while/switch/sizeof/... are matched earlier and win) or by className's 'new Foo(' rule (a constructor call is still a type reference). Covers both declarations (func Name() / entry func Main()) and call sites (Console.PrintLine(), risky()).
functionName.name: entity.name.function.gata

plainIdentifier.comment: Any identifier that matched nothing more specific — a variable or parameter. Colored identically to the editor's default foreground, but as an EXPLICIT scope rather than an absence of one: without it, an identifier inside a string.interpolated.gata hole (${...}) would inherit the string's color instead of looking like ordinary code.
plainIdentifier.name: variable.other.gata

punctuation.comment: Semicolons are their own scope (neutral, like operators). Parens/brackets go through #brackets (yellow, grey if an empty pair). Comma/dot/braces share one remaining neutral grey scope.
